message: docs: redesign iterative development around behavior evidence

files.patch:
@@ -0,0 +1,691 @@
+# Iterative-Development Redesign for Behavior Evidence
+
+## Goal
+
+Redesign the iterative-development skills so they continuously build and maintain a reusable black-box behavior corpus instead of treating end-to-end verification as a final audit activity.
+
+The new system should optimize for proving real behavior, not just for extracting stories, implementing code, and reviewing diffs.
+
+## Why This Exists
+
+The current iterative-development flow is strong at:
+
+- turning prose specs into story cards
+- decomposing an iteration into TDD-sized implementation tasks
+- reviewing code for story compliance and code quality
+
+That is necessary, but it is not sufficient.
+
+A product can still fail in real use when all of the following are true:
+
+- the requirement was extracted correctly
+- the story was assigned to an iteration
+- the implementation looks plausible in code review
+- unit or integration tests pass
+
+This happens when the process does not force the team to define and accumulate durable evidence for externally observable behavior.
+
+In practice, the current system conflates three different things:
+
+1. requirement coverage
+2. implementation coverage
+3. behavior evidence
+
+Those are related, but they are not the same.
+
+## The Core Problem
+
+### Requirement coverage
+
+A normative spec statement is represented by one or more story cards.
+
+This answers:
+
+- "Did we capture the requirement at all?"
+
+### Implementation coverage
+
+There is code and some test coverage that appears to satisfy the story.
+
+This answers:
+
+- "Did we build something for the story?"
+
+### Behavior evidence
+
+There is repeatable, externally meaningful evidence that the behavior actually works at the seam where a user, operator, or external system would detect failure.
+
+This answers:
+
+- "If this regresses in the real product, do we have a test that would catch it?"
+
+The current skills are optimized for the first two. They are not optimized enough for the third.
+
+That leads to predictable failure modes:
+
+- a bad spec requirement is preserved faithfully and shipped
+- a correct story is implemented, but the wrong seam is tested
+- a real assembled-system regression survives because the tests never exercised the observable behavior
+- a story marked done gets only light regression checking in later iterations, so behavior drifts
+
+## Preferred Language
+
+For this redesign, prefer the following terms:
+
+- `behavior evidence`: repeatable proof that a requirement holds at the correct seam
+- `black-box behavior corpus`: the reusable collection of externally observable checks
+- `proof seam`: the level where a requirement is verified
+- `scenario`: a reusable acceptance specification for observable behavior
+
+Avoid centering the redesign around the phrase `end-to-end testing`.
+
+End-to-end tests matter, but they are only one part of the behavior corpus. The corpus should include the cheapest seam that can actually falsify the requirement:
+
+- unit
+- integration
+- app-level black-box
+- full end-to-end
+
+The goal is not "more E2E." The goal is "the right black-box evidence, added continuously."
+
+## Engineering Principles
+
+### 1. The behavior corpus must grow continuously
+
+Behavior checks are not a final ceremony at the end of an iteration.
+
+Whenever an iteration changes externally observable behavior, that iteration must also add or extend the reusable checks that prove the behavior.
+
+### 2. Every externally observable requirement needs planned behavior evidence
+
+If a requirement can be observed from outside the implementation boundary, it needs a declared proof obligation before coding starts.
+
+Examples of externally observable behavior include:
+
+- UI state and layout
+- command behavior
+- menu items and their effects
+- startup and shutdown
+- relaunch behavior
+- persistence
+- download and caching behavior
+- permission and recovery flows
+- error handling visible to users or operators
+- interactions across processes or OS services
+
+### 3. Use the cheapest seam that can falsify the requirement
+
+Not every requirement needs a full end-to-end test.
+
+The correct question is:
+
+- "What is the cheapest test seam that would fail if the behavior were wrong in a way users would notice?"
+
+Examples:
+
+- a pure parser or formatter can be proven at unit level
+- a persistence contract can be proven with an integration test
+- a menu action, window lifecycle, or startup path usually needs app-level or end-to-end black-box evidence
+- a permission flow or relaunch path usually needs a real system or app-bundle seam, not just in-process tests
+
+### 4. Stories are not enough; scenarios are first-class
+
+Stories are good at expressing implementable work.
+
+They are weaker at expressing assembled behavior across boundaries.
+
+The redesigned system should extract and maintain both:
+
+- stories for implementation planning
+- scenarios for behavior evidence
+
+### 5. "Manual verification" is a debt marker, not a stopping point
+
+If the spec says `manual verification`, the skills should not treat that as a permanent test strategy.
+
+They should instead ask:
+
+- what portion of this can be automated now?
+- what seam would make it automatable?
+- what residual manual step remains, and why?
+
+Manual verification is acceptable only as an explicitly tracked residual risk.
+
+### 6. Audits must evaluate evidence quality, not just code existence
+
+An audit should not stop at:
+
+- code exists
+- tests exist
+- tests pass
+
+It must also ask:
+
+- do these tests prove the right behavior?
+- is the proof seam strong enough?
+- is the scenario reusable for future iterations?
+
+## Target Operating Model
+
+The redesigned workflow should look like this:
+
+1. Extract requirements into story cards and scenario cards.
+2. Assign each acceptance criterion a proof obligation.
+3. Plan iterations against both implementation scope and scenario closure.
+4. Implement code and add or extend behavior evidence in the same iteration.
+5. Audit the delivered behavior evidence and rerun the relevant black-box corpus.
+6. Declare completion only when the spec is represented by passing behavior evidence, not merely by completed stories.
+
+This model separates the roles cleanly:
+
+- `story cards` answer "what work should be built?"
+- `scenario cards` answer "what observable behavior must remain true?"
+- `proof obligations` answer "how will we know this requirement is actually satisfied?"
+
+## Proposed Artifacts
+
+This redesign needs three explicit artifacts.
+
+### 1. Story cards with proof obligations
+
+Keep story cards as the planning unit, but extend them.
+
+Each story should include:
+
+- `Story ID`
+- `Epic`
+- `Title`
+- `Sources`
+- `Acceptance criteria`
+- `Behavioral impact`: `none`, `local`, `cross-surface`, or `journey`
+- `Proof obligations`
+- `Scenario refs`
+
+Recommended structure:
+
+```md
+**Story:** STORY-0123
+**Epic:** EPIC-004 — Settings
+**Title:** Open settings as a reusable configuration window
+
+**Behavioral impact:** cross-surface
+
+**Acceptance criteria:**
+- AC-1: Selecting `Settings...` opens the settings window.
+- AC-2: The settings window title is `Ghost Pepper Settings`.
+- AC-3: Reopening settings reuses the existing window.
+
+**Proof obligations:**
+- AC-1: black-box app-level scenario required (`SCENARIO-0042`)
+- AC-2: black-box app-level scenario required (`SCENARIO-0042`)
+- AC-3: app-level scenario required (`SCENARIO-0043`)
+
+**Scenario refs:**
+- SCENARIO-0042
+- SCENARIO-0043
+```
+
+The important change is that a story no longer says only what must be built. It also says what evidence class is required before the story can be done.
+
+### 2. Scenario cards for reusable black-box behavior
+
+Add a scenario artifact with stable IDs. Scenarios are independent from stories because multiple stories may contribute to one scenario, and one story may require multiple scenarios.
+
+Use a single file at first to avoid premature complexity:
+
+- `docs/superpowers/iterations/behavior-scenarios.md`
+
+If the file later becomes unwieldy, split it by surface or journey. Do not introduce sharding until it is clearly needed.
+
+Each scenario should include:
+
+- `Scenario ID`
+- `Title`
+- `Kind`: `surface`, `journey`, `failure-recovery`, `contract`
+- `Sources`
+- `Owning stories`
+- `Preconditions`
+- `Action`
+- `Expected observables`
+- `Proof seam`
+- `Automation status`
+- `Execution command or harness`
+- `Notes / residual manual risk`
+
+Recommended structure:
+
+```md
+## SCENARIO-0042 — Open settings from the app menu
+
+**Kind:** surface
+**Proof seam:** app-level black-box
+**Owning stories:** STORY-0123
+
+**Preconditions:**
+- app launched
+- onboarding completed or bypassed by fixture
+
+**Action:**
+- open the app menu
+- choose `Settings...`
+
+**Expected observables:**
+- a window titled `Ghost Pepper Settings` appears
+- the process remains alive
+- the window size is 960x720 or larger than the defined minimum
+
+**Automation status:** automated
+**Execution command:** `scripts/run-smoke-suite.sh settings`
+
+**Sources:**
+- `specs/...`
+```
+
+Scenarios are the core of the black-box corpus.
+
+### 3. Behavior corpus index
+
+Add a lightweight index that answers:
+
+- which scenarios exist?
+- what seam do they run at?
+- when should they run?
+- which stories do they protect?
+
+Recommended path:
+
+- `docs/superpowers/iterations/behavior-corpus.md`
+
+Recommended fields per scenario:
+
+- `Scenario ID`
+- `Surface / journey`
+- `Proof seam`
+- `Run cadence`: `task`, `iteration`, `sentinel`, `full`
+- `Execution command`
+- `Owning stories`
+
+This artifact lets the auditor and orchestrator reason about behavior coverage directly instead of inferring it from code or prose.
+
+## What the Skills Should Optimize For
+
+The redesigned skills should optimize for this invariant:
+
+> An externally observable behavior is not done until it has durable, reusable evidence at the correct seam.
+
+That changes the meaning of "complete":
+
+- `implemented` means code exists
+- `tested` means some checks ran
+- `done` means the right evidence exists and is part of the reusable corpus
+
+## Prescriptive Skill Changes
+
+### Extracting Requirements
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/extracting-requirements/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/extracting-requirements/extraction-subagent-prompt.md`
+- any aggregation or validation scripts used by extraction
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+Extraction currently produces stories and a chunk coverage ledger. That is useful, but it does not force the system to extract reusable behavior scenarios or proof obligations.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+Extraction should emit both:
+
+- implementation-facing story cards
+- behavior-facing scenario cards
+
+For each normative chunk, the extractor should identify:
+
+- implementable responsibilities
+- externally observable behaviors
+- appropriate proof seams
+
+#### New extraction rules
+
+1. If a chunk contains externally observable behavior, extraction is incomplete unless there is at least one corresponding scenario.
+2. If a story has externally observable ACs, those ACs must declare proof obligations.
+3. If the source says `manual verification`, the extractor must still emit:
+   - a scenario card if the behavior is observable
+   - an automation target if plausible
+   - a residual manual-risk note only if automation is genuinely not feasible yet
+4. Coverage review must check both:
+   - requirement coverage
+   - scenario coverage
+
+#### New hard gate
+
+Do not proceed when a normative chunk has:
+
+- story coverage but no scenario coverage for observable behavior
+- scenario coverage but no declared proof seam
+
+#### Result
+
+Extraction output becomes the first place where behavior evidence is designed, not a later improvisation.
+
+### Scoping the Simplest Core
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/scoping-the-simplest-core/SKILL.md`
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+The walking skeleton is described as an end-to-end slice, but the skill does not require the slice to produce durable black-box scenarios.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+The walking skeleton must prove two things:
+
+1. the product exists
+2. the product has the first reusable behavior checks that future work can extend
+
+#### New scoping rules
+
+When selecting the walking skeleton:
+
+- include at least one scenario per core surface that makes the product recognizable
+- include at least one full journey scenario, even if it is thin
+- prefer stories that close scenario loops over stories that only add isolated internals
+
+The walking skeleton should leave behind:
+
+- the first stable scenario IDs
+- the first executable behavior harnesses
+- a small sentinel corpus that can be rerun every iteration
+
+### Running an Iteration
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/running-an-iteration/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/running-an-iteration/scope-reviewer-prompt.md`
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+Iteration planning decomposes story scope into TDD tasks, but it does not explicitly require evidence-building tasks or scenario closure.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+Before implementation starts, the planner should identify:
+
+- which existing scenarios are impacted
+- which new scenarios must be added
+- which proof obligations remain unfulfilled
+
+#### New planning rules
+
+Every iteration plan should contain both:
+
+- code tasks
+- evidence tasks
+
+Evidence tasks include:
+
+- adding or updating scenario cards
+- adding or extending black-box harnesses
+- strengthening weak seams when a unit test is insufficient
+
+#### Required scope reviewer checks
+
+The scope reviewer should evaluate:
+
+- whether the selected stories close the needed scenarios
+- whether the iteration leaves observable behavior without planned evidence
+- whether the iteration relies on end-of-iteration testing instead of building the corpus during the work
+
+#### New wrap-up gate
+
+Before the iteration returns to audit, it should verify:
+
+- every changed observable story has its declared evidence
+- every impacted scenario artifact was updated
+- the planned behavior commands actually ran
+
+### Implementing Tasks
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/implementing-tasks/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/implementing-tasks/implementer-subagent-prompt.md`
+- `skills/implementing-tasks/spec-compliance-reviewer-prompt.md`
+- `skills/implementing-tasks/code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md`
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+The implementer is told to use TDD and avoid fake tests. The reviewer is told to compare code to the task. Neither is explicitly required to build or inspect a reusable black-box scenario corpus.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+Implementation tasks should be framed as:
+
+- implement the behavior
+- attach durable evidence for the behavior
+
+#### Implementer prompt changes
+
+Before coding, require the implementer to state:
+
+- which ACs affect observable behavior
+- which proof seam each AC requires
+- which existing scenario will be extended, or which new scenario will be added
+
+Completion is invalid if:
+
+- observable behavior changed
+- but no scenario or harness was added or updated
+
+#### Spec-compliance reviewer changes
+
+The reviewer should not ask only:
+
+- "Does the code match the task?"
+
+It must also ask:
+
+- "Does the evidence prove the behavior at the right seam?"
+
+For externally observable ACs, the reviewer should reject:
+
+- unit-only evidence when the failure would only be visible at app or journey level
+- inspection-only claims without a strong reason
+- one-off manual test notes that did not update the corpus
+
+#### Code-quality reviewer changes
+
+The quality reviewer should inspect the corpus contribution itself:
+
+- is the new scenario clear and reusable?
+- is the harness narrowly scoped and maintainable?
+- does the implementation box future evidence into a brittle seam?
+
+### Auditing Progress
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/auditing-progress/SKILL.md`
+- `skills/auditing-progress/auditor-subagent-prompt.md`
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+The current audit performs deep review for just-finished stories and only a light sweep for previously completed work. That is not enough for a persistent behavior corpus.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+The audit should verify:
+
+- current iteration stories
+- current iteration scenario changes
+- impacted existing scenarios
+- a stable sentinel corpus of important product behaviors
+
+#### New audit tiers
+
+Use three tiers instead of two:
+
+1. `Deep evidence tier`
+   - stories completed this iteration
+   - scenarios added or changed this iteration
+   - proof obligations attached to those stories
+
+2. `Impacted behavior tier`
+   - all existing scenarios whose surfaces were touched by this iteration
+
+3. `Sentinel corpus tier`
+   - a small, stable set of high-value black-box scenarios rerun every iteration
+
+The sentinel corpus exists to catch regressions in older behavior even when the corresponding stories were completed several iterations ago.
+
+#### Auditor prompt changes
+
+Auditors should explicitly verify:
+
+- which scenario cards changed
+- whether the declared proof seam is appropriate
+- whether the scenario command actually proves the expected observables
+- whether inspection-only evidence is justified
+- whether any story marked done still lacks durable behavior evidence
+
+An iteration is not clean if:
+
+- the code passes but the evidence is too weak
+- a changed observable behavior has no corpus update
+- the sentinel corpus catches a regression
+
+### Iterative Development Orchestrator
+
+Affected files:
+
+- `skills/iterative-development/SKILL.md`
+
+#### Current weakness
+
+The completion gate focuses on whether the product covers the spec and whether stories are implemented. It does not treat the behavior corpus as the main proof object.
+
+#### Required redesign
+
+The orchestrator should consider the project complete only when:
+
+- normative spec content has both story coverage and behavior coverage
+- all proof obligations are satisfied
+- the relevant corpus passes
+- any remaining manual-only verification debt is explicit, justified, and accepted
+
+The final question should become:
+
+- "Can the system point to passing behavior evidence for the product surfaces the spec describes?"
+
+Not:
+
+- "Are the stories done?"
+
+## Suggested Prompt Deltas
+
+The skills author does not need to invent this from scratch. The following prompt changes should be made explicitly.
+
+### Add to implementer prompt
+
+```md
+Before you write code, map each AC to:
+- proof seam
+- test or harness to add or extend
+- scenario ID to update or create
+
+If the task changes observable behavior and you do not update the reusable
+behavior corpus, the task is incomplete.
+```
+
+### Add to spec-compliance reviewer prompt
+
+```md
+Do not accept externally observable ACs as satisfied unless the evidence is
+at the cheapest seam that could actually falsify the behavior.
+
+Reject:
+- unit-only proof for app-level behavior
+- inspection-only proof without justification
+- one-time manual verification that did not update the corpus
+```
+
+### Add to auditor prompt
+
+```md
+Audit proof obligations and scenario coverage, not just code and tests.
+
+For each changed observable story:
+- identify its scenario refs
+- run or inspect the declared evidence
+- verify the proof seam is strong enough
+- fail the audit if durable behavior evidence is missing
+```
+
+### Add to scope reviewer prompt
+
+```md
+Check whether the iteration leaves any externally observable behavior without
+planned scenario coverage or proof obligations.
+```
+
+## Recommended File and Artifact Layout
+
+Keep this redesign simple.
+
+Recommended initial layout:
+
+- `docs/superpowers/iterations/requirements/EPIC-*.md`
+  - extended with proof obligations and scenario refs
+- `docs/superpowers/iterations/behavior-scenarios.md`
+  - scenario cards with stable IDs
+- `docs/superpowers/iterations/behavior-corpus.md`
+  - execution index and run cadence
+
+Do not create a large hierarchy of scenario files or execution metadata until the simpler version becomes painful.
+
+## Adoption Order
+
+This project is still in development, so no migration strategy is needed.
+
+The skills author should implement the redesign in this order:
+
+1. extend extraction to produce proof obligations and scenarios
+2. add the new scenario and corpus artifacts
+3. update iteration planning to include evidence tasks
+4. update implementer and reviewer prompts to require corpus updates
+5. update auditing to inspect proof quality and rerun impacted plus sentinel scenarios
+6. update orchestrator completion gates to require passing behavior coverage
+
+This order matters because later stages depend on the earlier artifact model.
+
+## Non-Goals
+
+This redesign does not mean:
+
+- every AC must become a full end-to-end test
+- unit and integration tests become less important
+- all manual verification disappears immediately
+- every iteration must run a huge, slow product-wide suite
+
+The goal is not maximal test quantity. The goal is durable proof at the right seam.
+
+## What Success Looks Like
+
+This redesign is successful when the iterative-development skills can do all of the following:
+
+- extract not just stories, but also reusable behavior scenarios
+- require proof obligations before implementation starts
+- grow the black-box behavior corpus during normal iteration work
+- reject story completion when observable behavior lacks durable evidence
+- catch regressions in older product behavior through a stable sentinel corpus
+- make it obvious whether a failure came from:
+  - a bad spec
+  - a missing story
+  - weak implementation
+  - weak evidence
+
+Most importantly, the process should leave behind a better product and a better test corpus after every iteration, not just more completed stories.
